This case was investigated by the FBI, the Debuke Police Department and the Grant County Sheriff's office. The two females lied about their ages and were actually 14 and 15 years old. After thorough investigation, detectives found that Raymond Armor, the male the two females were with, took inappropriate photos of them and posted them online. Raymond then attempted to arrange meetings between the females and interested individuals. None of the meetings occurred, however. Raymond was charged with two counts of transporting an individual in interstate commerce with the intent to engage in inappropriate services. He pleaded guilty to both charges on July 18th, 2024 and was sentenced to 11 years in federal prison. This suspect's name is Bryce Witchy. His warrant was for felony telecommunications harassment and importuning for attempting to engage in inappropriate activity with an 8-year-old girl. In addition to his warrant, he was charged with obstructing official business and resisting arrest. After looking into Bryce's past, he was also later charged with 30 additional counts of illegal use of a minor or impaired person in inappropriate material. Bryce pleaded guilty to all counts on July 31st, 2025 and was sentenced to 19 and a half years in prison. We are dealing with a kid who's been through a lot and you automatically become the enemy. That's why the first thing foster or adopted parents has to learn is how to deal with being hated by the very child they're trying to save. That same dynamic plays out on the streets when officers meet runaways. When kids have been manipulated or exploited, their sense of loyalty gets rewired and they start to associate attention, even the wrong kind with love. But you show up trying to save a kid who's been manipulated and instead of gratitude, you might get screams, curses or maybe even a swing. That's trauma talking, not disrespect. Kids like this don't think you're rescuing them. They think you're arresting their only friend. That's the twisted psychology these people create. They convince these young girls that love looks like control and attention equals affection. Often these guys don't have to drag anyone away by force because they do it mentally. And while they set up cameras and attempt to cross state lines, they also sell the idea that they're partners, not victims. That's what makes these people so dangerous because they weaponize hope. Foster parents, cops and social workers all end up in the same fight just wearing different badges. They're trying to get through to a kid who's been burned so many times they don't recognize someone who cares when it's real. This is why saving someone isn't always about heroics, it's about standing firm even when they hate you for helping them.
